questions.question_text: An agent's listing agreement expires. Must the agent still protect the former client's confidential information?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, confidentiality survives termination of the relationship
distractors: No, all duties end when the agreement expires
Only for 90 days after the agreement ends
Only if the client requests it in writing
questions.explanation: In Virginia, the duty of confidentiality survives the termination of the brokerage relationship. An agent must continue to protect a former client's confidential information indefinitely.

questions.question_text: Which duty requires a Virginia agent to inform clients of material adverse facts even when not asked?
distractors: Obedience
Loyalty
Accounting
questions.explanation: The duty of disclosure requires Virginia agents to proactively inform their clients of known material adverse facts that could affect the transaction, regardless of whether the client asks.

questions.question_text: A seller instructs her agent to withhold a known foundation problem from buyers. What should the agent do?
questions.correct_answer: Refuse and disclose the material adverse fact
distractors: Follow the instruction under the duty of obedience
Refer the matter to the principal broker only
Withdraw from the listing without any disclosure
questions.explanation: An agent cannot follow instructions that violate the law. The duty to disclose material adverse facts overrides obedience, and the agent must reveal the foundation problem to prospective buyers.

questions.question_text: Which set correctly lists all standard agent fiduciary duties in Virginia?
questions.correct_answer: Loyalty, obedience, confidentiality, accounting, care, disclosure
distractors: Loyalty, honesty, competence, fairness, good faith
Obedience, accounting, marketing, negotiation, care
Confidentiality, disclosure, appraisal, inspection, loyalty
questions.explanation: Virginia standard agents owe six fiduciary duties: loyalty, obedience, confidentiality, accounting, reasonable care and diligence, and disclosure of material adverse facts.

questions.question_text: A buyer's agent discovers a neighbor plans to open a noisy auto shop next door. The buyer has not asked about the neighborhood. Must the agent disclose this?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, it is a material adverse fact requiring disclosure
distractors: No, the buyer did not specifically ask about it
Only if the seller's agent confirms the information
Only after the buyer makes an offer on the property
questions.explanation: Virginia agents must disclose known material adverse facts to their clients regardless of whether the client inquires. A planned noisy business next door qualifies as a material adverse fact.

questions.question_text: A Virginia agent works with an unrepresented buyer who is a customer. Which duty does the agent owe this buyer?
questions.correct_answer: Honesty and disclosure of material adverse facts
distractors: Full fiduciary duties including loyalty
Obedience to the buyer's instructions
Confidentiality regarding the buyer's finances
questions.explanation: In Virginia, agents owe customers (non-clients) honesty, disclosure of material adverse facts, and accounting for any money handled. They do not owe fiduciary duties like loyalty or confidentiality.

questions.question_text: A listing agent receives earnest money from an unrepresented buyer. Must the agent account for these funds?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, accounting for money is owed to all parties
distractors: No, accounting is only owed to clients
Only if the amount exceeds $5,000
Only if the buyer requests a written receipt
questions.explanation: Virginia requires agents to account for all money received, regardless of whether the person is a client or customer. This duty applies to all parties in a transaction.

questions.question_text: An agent representing the seller learns the property has a leaking roof. An unrepresented buyer is touring the home. Must the agent tell the buyer?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, material adverse facts must be disclosed to all parties
distractors: No, the agent only owes duties to the seller client
Only if the buyer has a home inspection contingency
Only if the seller authorizes the disclosure
questions.explanation: Virginia law requires disclosure of known material adverse facts to all parties, including customers. The leaking roof is a material adverse fact that must be disclosed to the unrepresented buyer.

questions.question_text: What distinguishes the duties owed to a client from those owed to a customer in Virginia?
questions.correct_answer: Clients receive full fiduciary duties; customers do not
distractors: Clients pay higher commission rates than customers
Customers receive written disclosures; clients do not
Clients can sue for breach; customers cannot
questions.explanation: The key distinction is fiduciary obligation. Clients receive the full range of fiduciary duties (loyalty, obedience, confidentiality, etc.) while customers receive only honesty, material fact disclosure, and accounting.

questions.question_text: A buyer customer tells the listing agent her maximum budget is $400,000. Must the agent keep this confidential?
questions.correct_answer: No, confidentiality is not owed to customers
distractors: Yes, all financial information is always confidential
Yes, for 30 days after the conversation
Only if the buyer asks the agent not to share it
questions.explanation: In Virginia, the duty of confidentiality is owed only to clients, not customers. The listing agent could share the buyer customer's budget information with the seller without violating any duty.

questions.question_text: A listing agent knows the basement floods every spring but says nothing to buyers. What violation has occurred?
questions.correct_answer: Misrepresentation by omission of a material fact
distractors: No violation since the agent made no false statement
A minor disclosure technicality only
Commingling of escrow funds
questions.explanation: In Virginia, omitting a known material fact is treated the same as affirmative misrepresentation. The agent's failure to disclose the flooding constitutes misrepresentation by omission.

questions.question_text: What type of conduct is prohibited under Virginia's substantial misrepresentation rules?
questions.correct_answer: Making false statements about material property facts
distractors: Expressing personal opinions about market trends
Recommending a preferred home inspector
Advertising a property below market value
questions.explanation: Virginia prohibits substantial misrepresentation, which includes making false statements about material facts related to properties or transactions. Opinions and recommendations are generally not misrepresentation.

questions.question_text: A seller instructs her agent to tell buyers the septic system is new, but the agent knows it is 15 years old. What must the agent do?
questions.correct_answer: Refuse the instruction and disclose the true age
distractors: Follow the seller's instruction under duty of obedience
Say nothing and let the buyer's inspector find it
Refer the buyer to the county records office
questions.explanation: Virginia agents cannot follow unlawful instructions. Misrepresenting the age of the septic system would be substantial misrepresentation. The agent must refuse and disclose the accurate information.

questions.question_text: After both parties sign a purchase agreement, when must the agent deliver copies to the parties?
distractors: Within 10 business days of execution
At the final settlement meeting only
Only upon written request from a party
questions.explanation: Virginia requires agents to promptly deliver copies of all executed documents to the parties and to leave copies with the appropriate individuals. Delay in document delivery is a violation.

questions.question_text: A buyer's agent knows a property has termite damage but the agent represents a different buyer interested in the same home. Must the agent disclose the damage to the other buyer?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, material adverse facts must be disclosed regardless
distractors: No, the agent only owes duties to their own client
Only if the other buyer is also the agent's client
Only if the listing agent fails to disclose first
questions.explanation: Virginia requires agents to disclose known material adverse facts to all parties, regardless of whom they represent. The termite damage is a material adverse fact that must be shared with any interested buyer.
